channel.title: My Research Feed
channel.description: Deep research briefings powered by Claude

item.title: Claude Opus 4.7 ships with sharper coding, 3x vision resolution, and a new xhigh effort tier
item.description:
Anthropic shipped Claude Opus 4.7 on April 16, 2026, slotting in above Opus 4.6 with the same $5 / $25 per million input/output token pricing and the same model family but a notably stiffer set of capabilities for agentic coding, computer use, and long-context document work. The model is live on the Claude Platform, Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, and Microsoft Foundry under the API ID claude-opus-4-7, with availability starting on day one rather than the staggered rollouts that shipped earlier 4.x models.

The headline numbers come from partner evaluations rather than the usual public leaderboards. Anthropic cites a 13% improvement over Opus 4.6 on coding benchmarks from partner testing, 3x more production tasks resolved on Rakuten-SWE-Bench, and a 21% reduction in document reasoning errors on OfficeQA Pro. The most striking jump is on computer-use evaluations: visual acuity climbed from 54.5% on Opus 4.6 to 98.5% on Opus 4.7. Anthropic attributes this to expanded vision input — images up to 2,576 pixels on the long edge (~3.75 megapixels), more than 3x the previous cap — which lets the model actually read dense screenshots rather than guessing at them. The same vision overhaul drives improvements on chemical structures, technical diagrams, and the kind of cluttered enterprise UIs that have historically broken computer-use agents.

Three under-the-hood changes matter for engineers running production stacks today. First, a new xhigh effort level joins the existing low/medium/high knobs, giving finer control over the reasoning-vs-latency tradeoff for tasks that genuinely need more thinking time — coding agents, multi-step research, and long-running computer-use sessions are the obvious targets. Second, the tokenizer was updated; the same English text now maps to 1.0–1.35x more tokens than under Opus 4.6, which means existing budget calculations need to be refreshed before any cost-comparison work. Third, "task budgets" enter public beta, giving callers a way to cap a turn's reasoning spend without juggling raw token math — a meaningful operational improvement for teams running large fleets of agent calls.

Anthropic also ships file-system memory improvements that span sessions, and the company explicitly warns that instruction adherence is tighter — prompts tuned against Opus 4.6 may behave differently and need retuning. That's a recurring theme with Anthropic releases: each generation gets more literal about following directives, which rewards specific prompts and punishes vague ones. Anthropic's own guidance suggests treating Opus 4.7 as a soft-breaking change in prompt behavior even though the API surface is identical.

On safety, Opus 4.7 holds the same Responsible Scaling Policy classification as 4.6, with claimed gains in honesty and prompt-injection resistance. Cybersecurity capabilities were intentionally suppressed in training; security researchers who need raw capability can apply to Anthropic's Cyber Verification Program for elevated access — an unusual gating model that other labs have not adopted, and one that reflects Anthropic's broader bet on capability-conditioned access rather than universal restriction.

Two pieces of context are worth flagging. First, this release is the engine inside Claude Design, the new visual creation product Anthropic launched the day after on April 17 — Opus 4.7's vision improvements are precisely what makes that product viable. Second, with GPT-5.5 ($5 / $30) shipping a week later on April 23 and DeepSeek V4-Pro ($1.74 / $3.48) the day after that, Opus 4.7 sits in an interesting middle position: more expensive than DeepSeek by 3x, slightly cheaper than GPT-5.5 on output, and aimed squarely at customers who care more about reliability on long agentic tasks than headline benchmarks. Anthropic claims Opus 4.7 leads on the new GDPval-AA economic-task evaluation, though independent third-party numbers will take a few weeks to filter in.

The release also lines up with Anthropic's revenue trajectory: run-rate revenue passed $30 billion this month, up from $9 billion in late 2025, per the same quarter's Amazon compute deal disclosure. Opus is increasingly the workhorse model for that enterprise base, not a research showcase, and the 4.7 release reflects that — incremental on raw intelligence, meaningful on the operational characteristics (vision reach, instruction following, budget controls) that determine whether a model survives in production.

For developers already on Opus 4.6: the API drop-in is clean, but plan a token-budget audit and a prompt regression pass before flipping production traffic. The xhigh tier is worth experimenting with on tasks that previously hit ceiling quality at high effort, and the new vision resolution justifies revisiting any computer-use workflows that struggled with screenshot fidelity.

Sources:

  • https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-opus-4-7
  • https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-design-anthropic-labs
  • https://www.anthropic.com/news/anthropic-amazon-compute

item.title: OpenAI ships GPT-5.5 six weeks after 5.4, leaning hard into agents and Codex
item.description:
OpenAI released GPT-5.5 on April 23, 2026, an unusually fast turnaround — just six weeks after GPT-5.4 — that frames the new model less as a generational jump and more as a continuous-delivery checkpoint aimed squarely at competitive pressure from Anthropic and Google. GPT-5.5 and a heavier GPT-5.5 Pro tier are now rolling out to ChatGPT Plus, Pro, Business, and Enterprise users plus Codex; both reached the API on April 24 the day after the consumer launch.

The company's framing is explicit: GPT-5.5 is "a faster, sharper thinker for fewer tokens" than 5.4. The interesting word there is fewer. OpenAI is claiming per-token latency parity with 5.4 while delivering meaningfully higher capability, which translates to lower effective cost on agentic workloads where the model previously had to think longer to reach a comparable answer. The gains are concentrated in four areas the company calls out by name: agentic coding, computer use, knowledge work, and early scientific research — anywhere progress depends on reasoning across context and taking action over time, rather than one-shot question answering.

On benchmarks, OpenAI claims GPT-5.5 "consistently scores higher" than Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.5 across published evaluations. The fact that the comparison cites Opus 4.5 rather than the just-shipped Opus 4.7 is conspicuous — Anthropic shipped 4.7 on April 16, a week before GPT-5.5's launch — and reinforces the pattern of every lab now picking the most favorable cross-comparison available. Independent benchmark numbers from third parties like Artificial Analysis and LiveBench will be the more useful signal over the next two weeks.

The agentic story is where GPT-5.5 puts most of its weight. OpenAI describes the model as able to "carry more of the work itself" and "move across tools until a task is finished" — phrasing that lines up with the model's improvements on Codex (now substantially upgraded as the agentic-coding default) and on Workspace agents, OpenAI's new product surface for sustained multi-step work in spreadsheets, documents, and the browser. Workspace agents are free until May 6, 2026, after which credit-based pricing takes effect. That introductory window doubles as both a marketing push and a data-collection exercise; expect aggressive pricing announcements in early May once OpenAI sees real workload distribution.

Safety testing followed OpenAI's now-standard ramp: full Preparedness Framework evaluation, internal and external red-teamers, targeted advanced cybersecurity and biology testing, and feedback from "nearly 200 trusted early-access partners" before release. The release notes do not flag any new capability classifications under the framework, which is consistent with GPT-5.5 being an iterative refinement on 5.4's foundation rather than a frontier-pushing model.

For developers, the key operational details: GPT-5.5 maintains GPT-5.4's API contract, latency profile, and (per OpenAI) per-token serving cost in real-world traffic. The Pro variant offers stronger reasoning at higher cost, mirroring Anthropic's Opus/Sonnet tiering. Pricing is reportedly $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens — slightly above Claude Opus 4.7's $5 / $25 and dramatically above DeepSeek V4-Pro's $1.74 / $3.48 (which dropped the day after, on April 24). The market for the top tier of frontier models has compressed to a tight band on the Western side and a much cheaper Chinese alternative.

The strategic read: OpenAI is no longer trying to win individual benchmark fights. The six-week cadence between 5.4 and 5.5, the agent-first framing, the Codex investment, and the Workspace agents launch all point at treating ChatGPT as an operating layer rather than a chat product. As Fortune put it, model launches are starting to look like software updates — version numbers tick, capabilities accrete, and the conversation shifts from "is the new model smarter?" to "what new things can my agent do today that it couldn't yesterday?"

For teams already on the GPT-5 family: 5.5 should be a near-drop-in upgrade with measurable wins on long agentic workflows, especially anything involving Codex or computer-use scaffolding. The Pro tier is worth A/B testing on the hardest 10% of workloads where 5.4 hit reasoning ceilings.

Sources:

  • https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-5/
  • https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/23/openai-announces-latest-artificial-intelligence-model.html
  • https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/23/openai-chatgpt-gpt-5-5-ai-model-superapp/
  • https://fortune.com/2026/04/23/openai-releases-gpt-5-5/

item.title: Gemma 4 lands in four sizes, including a 26B MoE that activates only 3.8B parameters
item.description:
Google DeepMind released Gemma 4 in April 2026, refreshing its open-weights family with four variants that span from edge-class to near-frontier and adopt the mixture-of-experts trick that has come to dominate efficient open models. The release is Apache 2.0 licensed across all sizes — commercially permissive, no usage-based restrictions — and ships with day-one support across vLLM, llama.cpp, MLX, Ollama, NVIDIA NIM, LiteRT-LM, and roughly fifteen other inference frameworks.

The four variants stake out different tradeoffs:

  • Gemma 4 E2B — Effective 2B parameters, edge-optimized, 128K context window. Aimed at on-device inference on phones and laptops.
  • Gemma 4 E4B — Effective 4B parameters, also edge-optimized at 128K context. The sweet spot for consumer-grade GPUs.
  • Gemma 4 26B MoE — Mixture-of-experts architecture with 26B total parameters but only 3.8B activated per token, optimized for serving latency. 256K context.
  • Gemma 4 31B Dense — Full-parameter model targeting maximum quality and fine-tuning headroom. 256K context.

The 31B dense model lands at #3 on the Arena AI text leaderboard among open models, with the 26B MoE at #6 — both serious placings against a leaderboard increasingly populated by Chinese open-weights releases. Google's framing is "byte for byte, the most capable open models," with explicit claims of "outcompeting models 20x its size" on performance-per-parameter. That kind of claim is normally posturing, but the architectural details support it: Gemma 4 inherits research from Gemini 3, including the multimodal training pipeline that lets even the smallest variants accept native video, image, and audio input and produce structured JSON for agentic workflows.

Three things stand out for engineers looking to actually deploy these models. First, native function-calling and structured JSON output are baked in across all four variants — no fine-tuning required to use Gemma 4 as the brain of an agent loop. Second, the bfloat16 weights are explicitly tuned for both consumer GPUs and Google's own edge silicon (the company calls out Pixel collaboration), making this the most realistic open Google model yet for on-device deployment. Third, the model was "natively trained on over 140 languages," with stronger OCR and chart-understanding than Gemma 3 — a meaningful upgrade for non-English document workflows.

The MoE variant is the most interesting architectural choice. At 26B total / 3.8B active, it sits in the same activation-cost band as a 4B dense model while delivering meaningfully higher capability. That is the same playbook DeepSeek used to dominate the open-weights value curve last year, and Google's adoption signals a broader convergence on MoE for any open model targeting "good enough on a single GPU." For self-hosters, the 26B MoE on commodity 80GB hardware is the variant most worth benchmarking against alternatives like DeepSeek V3.2 and Qwen 3.

Gemma 4 also lands the same week Google DeepMind published its Decoupled DiLoCo paper, which describes the asynchronous distributed-training infrastructure that produced these models. The paper reports 88% goodput on simulated 1.2-million-chip runs versus 27% for conventional data-parallel training, and a wide-area bandwidth reduction from 198 Gbps to 0.84 Gbps across eight datacenters. The training method also successfully mixed TPU v6e and v5p generations in single runs — a quiet capability that materially extends the useful lifespan of older hardware. Gemma 4's 12B-parameter validation run trained more than 20x faster on this stack than on conventional data-parallel infrastructure while matching baseline accuracy (64.1% vs 64.4%).

The strategic context: open weights from Western labs has been a thin field. Meta's Llama family has lost momentum, Mistral's output has slowed in early 2026, and DeepSeek and Qwen have set the open-source quality bar. Google releasing a competitive open family with permissive licensing — and publishing the training infrastructure paper alongside it — is the most significant Western open-source push in months. For teams that want to fine-tune frontier-quality models without sending data to a hosted API, the 31B dense is now a real option. For inference-cost-constrained deployments, the 26B MoE deserves a head-to-head against DeepSeek V3.2 before May.

Sources:

  • https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/technology/developers-tools/gemma-4/
  • https://deepmind.google/blog/decoupled-diloco/

item.title: Meta launches Muse Spark, the first model from MSL, with a Contemplating multi-agent reasoning mode
item.description:
Meta unveiled Muse Spark on April 8, 2026, the first model from Meta Superintelligence Labs (MSL) and the company's explicit attempt to reset its position in the frontier race after a year in which Llama's momentum stalled and OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and DeepSeek pulled ahead. Meta is framing Muse Spark as "the first step on our scaling ladder" toward what it calls personal superintelligence, with availability immediately at meta.ai and the Meta AI app, and a private API preview for select developers.

The model is natively multimodal, supports tool use and visual chain of thought, and introduces a new test-time reasoning mode Meta calls Contemplating — parallel multi-agent collaboration where the model spawns and orchestrates multiple reasoning agents simultaneously rather than running a single linear chain-of-thought. On Humanity's Last Exam, Contemplating mode scores 58%; on FrontierScience Research it hits 38%. Both numbers are competitive with the top tier from other labs, though Meta's benchmark presentation makes direct comparisons hard — neither figure is reported alongside identical-protocol numbers from GPT-5.5 or Claude Opus 4.7.

Meta is being unusually specific about how it scaled Muse Spark, framing the work along three axes:

  • Pretraining efficiency: Meta claims Muse Spark achieves capabilities "over an order of magnitude" more efficiently than Llama 4 Maverick through architectural improvements and data curation. That is a significant claim — if it holds up, it implies Meta is no longer compute-bottlenecked but data-and-architecture-bottlenecked, which matches the broader industry shift.
  • Reinforcement learning: The team reports "log-linear growth" in RL post-training and predictable generalization to held-out tasks, which is the kind of scaling-law claim that other labs have made but rarely substantiated publicly.
  • Test-time reasoning: Contemplating mode is Meta's answer to OpenAI's o-series and Anthropic's extended thinking. Where those labs scaled single-trace reasoning, Meta is betting on parallel multi-agent reasoning at inference time.

Alongside the model, Meta published an updated Advanced AI Scaling Framework v2 and a Safety & Preparedness Report specific to Muse Spark. The notable methodological shift is in safety training: rather than rules-based refusal training, Meta translated safety guidelines into "clear, testable principles" and trained the model on why something is safe rather than just what to refuse. The framework still tests against thousands of adversarial scenarios across chemical/biological threats, cybersecurity, loss-of-control risks, ideological bias, and standard policy violations, both before and after safety protections are applied.

The naming and structure are worth dwelling on. By branding the family Muse (rather than Llama 5) and putting MSL on the door, Meta is signaling that this is a clean architectural break from the Llama lineage that defined the company's open-source AI strategy from 2023 through 2025. It also raises a question Meta hasn't answered: Muse Spark's weights are not open, and the announcement makes no commitment about future Muse-family models being released under permissive licensing. After three years of Meta's frontier work being substantially open, that is a meaningful inflection.

For developers: the API preview is the only path to programmatic access, and Meta has not published pricing or rate limits. Contemplating mode is rolling out gradually rather than to all users on day one, which is consistent with the high inference cost of multi-agent reasoning approaches at scale. The model is genuinely usable today through meta.ai for consumer-grade tasks, but enterprise integration timelines are unclear.

Two things to watch over the next month. First, whether independent benchmarks (Artificial Analysis, LiveBench, GPQA leaderboards) confirm Muse Spark's claimed parity with the GPT-5.5 / Opus 4.7 / Gemini 3.1 tier. Second, whether Meta releases any Muse weights — the company's open-source posture has been one of the clearest differentiators from OpenAI and Anthropic, and Muse Spark's closed launch suggests that may be changing. The combination of "personal superintelligence" framing and a closed launch is a sharper turn than Meta has telegraphed before, and worth tracking.

Sources:

  • https://ai.meta.com/blog/introducing-muse-spark-msl/
  • https://ai.meta.com/blog/scaling-how-we-build-test-advanced-ai/

item.title: DeepSeek V4 Pro and Flash arrive with 1.6T parameters, 1M context, and frontier pricing pressure
item.description:
DeepSeek released preview versions of V4 Pro and V4 Flash on April 24, 2026 — the long-awaited successor to the V3 family that defined the open-weights value curve through 2025. The release is MIT licensed, ships with a 1 million token context window on both variants, and prices the larger Pro model at $1.74 per million input tokens / $3.48 per million output. That undercuts Claude Opus 4.7 ($5 / $25) and GPT-5.5 ($5 / $30) by roughly 3x on input and 7-8x on output, while the Flash variant ($0.14 / $0.28) sits in a price tier where no Western frontier model competes.

The architectural specs are unusual:

  • V4-Pro: 1.6 trillion total parameters with 49 billion active per token via Mixture-of-Experts. The largest open-weights model released to date — past Kimi K2.6 and GLM-5.1.
  • V4-Flash: 284 billion total / 13 billion active. Built for high-throughput, low-latency serving.

The technical innovation DeepSeek emphasizes is Hybrid Attention Architecture, which the company says improves long-conversation memory and explicitly enables the 1M-token context. The V4 paper claims V4-Pro "attains only 27% of the single-token FLOPs and 10% of the KV cache size relative to DeepSeek-V3.2," which — if accurate — is the kind of efficiency leap that explains the aggressive pricing. The combination of MoE sparsity (49B active out of 1.6T) and a dramatically smaller KV cache footprint is what makes 1M-context serving economically viable at all on commodity infrastructure.

On benchmarks, DeepSeek claims V4-Pro-Max surpasses GPT-5.2 and Gemini 3.0 Pro on certain reasoning tasks, with coding "comparable to GPT-5.4." Knowledge tests still trail GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro, and DeepSeek itself acknowledges in the release that the trajectory "trails state-of-the-art frontier models by approximately 3 to 6 months" — a rare moment of public honesty about where Chinese open-weights stand relative to the Western frontier. Simon Willison's analysis frames it more bluntly: V4-Pro is "almost on the frontier, a fraction of the price."

The catch — and it is a real one — is that V4 is text-only. No vision, audio, or video input, which puts it out of contention for the multimodal agentic workloads that Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5 are explicitly built for. For pure-text reasoning, coding, document analysis, and agent loops where the model only needs to read and write text, V4 is now the most competitive offering on the cost curve. For computer use, screenshot reasoning, or any visual task, it is a non-starter.

What this means strategically. The 24-hour gap between GPT-5.5 (April 23) and DeepSeek V4 (April 24) was almost certainly not coincidence — DeepSeek timed the release to compress the news cycle and force direct comparison. Western labs have been racing on capability and absorbing the pricing pressure as a cost of doing business; DeepSeek V4 is a forcing function. With 1M context, MIT licensing, MoE efficiency, and roughly an order-of-magnitude price advantage, V4 will set the floor for any commercial conversation about long-context text reasoning for the rest of Q2.

For self-hosters, V4-Pro at 1.6T total parameters is genuinely large — the model needs serious infrastructure to run, and most teams will consume it through DeepSeek's hosted API rather than running weights locally. V4-Flash at 284B total / 13B active is the variant that fits realistic open-weights deployment scenarios, and it is the one to benchmark against Gemma 4 26B-MoE and Qwen 3 for self-hosted text workloads.

Bloomberg ran two pieces in 24 hours arguing both that V4 narrows the US-China AI gap and that it fails to do so — a useful reminder that "narrowing the gap" depends entirely on which axis you measure. On capability per dollar for text reasoning, the gap is essentially closed. On multimodal frontier capability, it isn't. DeepSeek's own framing acknowledges the latter; the next interesting question is whether V5 closes the multimodal gap or whether DeepSeek doubles down on the text-cost advantage.

Sources:

  • https://simonwillison.net/2026/Apr/24/deepseek-v4/
  • https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/24/deepseek-previews-new-ai-model-that-closes-the-gap-with-frontier-models/
  • https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/24/deepseek-v4-llm-preview-open-source-ai-competition-china.html
  • https://www.technologyreview.com/2026/04/24/1136422/why-deepseeks-v4-matters/
